are several places where we call `np.sort/argsort/etc.` internally, i.e. not cases where users can specify a sorting `kind` like in `sort_values`, and use the default unstable `kind="quicksort"`

In numpy 1.25, it appears that CPUs that can use AVX will have a modified quicksort and recently broke some tests xref https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/53548 in our numpy dev build where we were testing these unstable sorting results.

Is it worth transitioning to a stable sorting algorithm internally for consistency?

Alternatively we could dynamically transition to use a stable sorting algorithm if duplicate values are being 1.2

I'm testing the `plot_spiral_arm_models.py` and `plot_spiral_arms.py` example scripts located in https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/tree/f1c4ff6b32e2026b64c0609493c6b2240bb25caa/docs/user-guide/astro/population

The Vallee spiral arms start from the same positions when using either `radius` or `theta`in the `xy_position` method:

![Figure_1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f6310449-29f8-45ec-9ee0-318087fba302)


But the Faucher spiral arms do not:

![Figure_2](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a7fd0b04-2348-4879-b2d8-5d28bc61f071)

Am I doing something wrong?


```
import numpy as np
from astropy.units import Quantity
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from gammapy.astro.population.spatial import FaucherSpiral, ValleeSpiral


vallee_spiral = ValleeSpiral()
faucher_spiral = FaucherSpiral()
radius = Quantity(np.arange(2.1, 10, 0.1), "kpc")

# Vallee model
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(7, 8))
rect = [0.12, 0.12, 0.85, 0.85]
ax_cartesian = fig.add_axes(rect)
ax_cartesian.set_aspect("equal")

for spiralarm_index in range(4):
    color = "C{}".format(spiralarm_index)

    # Plot Vallee spiral using radius
    x, y = vallee_spiral.xy_position(radius=radius, spiralarm_index=spiralarm_index)
    name = vallee_spiral.spiralarms[spiralarm_index]
    ax_cartesian.plot(x, y, label="Vallee R " + name, ls="-", color=color)

    # Plot Vallee spiral using theta
    theta_0 = vallee_spiral.theta_0[spiralarm_index].value
    theta = Quantity(np.linspace(theta_0, theta_0 + 360, 100), 'deg')
    x, y = vallee_spiral.xy_position(theta=theta, spiralarm_index=spiralarm_index)
    name = vallee_spiral.spiralarms[spiralarm_index]
    ax_cartesian.plot(x.value, y.value, label="Vallee T " + name, ls="--", color='k')

ax_cartesian.set_xlim(-12, 12)
ax_cartesian.set_ylim(-15, 12)
ax_cartesian.legend(ncol=2, loc="lower right")
plt.show()

# Faucher model
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(7, 8))
rect = [0.12, 0.12, 0.85, 0.85]
ax_cartesian = fig.add_axes(rect)
ax_cartesian.set_aspect("equal")

for spiralarm_index in range(4):
    color = "C{}".format(spiralarm_index)

    # Plot Faucher spiral using radius
    x, y = faucher_spiral.xy_position(radius=radius, spiralarm_index=spiralarm_index)
    name = faucher_spiral.spiralarms[spiralarm_index]
    ax_cartesian.plot(x, y, label="Faucher R " + name, ls="-.", color=color)

    # Plot Faucher spiral using theta
    theta_0 = faucher_spiral.theta_0[spiralarm_index].value
    theta = Quantity(np.linspace(theta_0, theta_0 + 2 * np.pi, 100), "rad")
    x, y = faucher_spiral.xy_position(theta=theta, spiralarm_index=spiralarm_index)
    name = faucher_spiral.spiralarms[spiralarm_index]
    ax_cartesian.plot(x.value, y.value, label="Faucher T " + name, ls="--", color='k')

ax_cartesian.set_xlim(-12, 12)
ax_cartesian.set_ylim(-15, 12)
ax_cartesian.legend(ncol=2, loc="lower right")
plt.show()

According to [this post from 2018](https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/prev_whats_new/whats_new_2.2.html#new-style-colorblind-friendly-color-cycle)  `tableau-colorblind10t` is the colorblind friendly cycle and `Cividis` is the colorblind friendly colormap. But if I use the `tableau-colorblind10t` style the colormap used by default is not `Cividis`.

Shouldn't it be?

### Code for reproduction

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.style.use('tableau-colorblind10')

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
pcm = ax.pcolormesh(np.random.rand(32,32))
fig.colorbar(pcm)
plt.show()
```

Description

See issue title

### Expected behavior

Units are taken into account correctly

### How to Reproduce

```python
import astropy.units as u
from astropy.coordinates.angle_utilities import angular_separation
from astropy.table import Table


t = Table({
    'lon1': [1, 2] * u.deg,
    'lat1': [1, 2] * u.deg,
    'lon2': [0, 0] * u.deg,
    'lat2': [50, 50] * u.deg,
})

The delimiter should be correctly guessed

### Description
Defining a `SkyCoord`  object using a `pandas` dataframe is hundreds of times slower than simply using a `numpy` array

### Expected behavior
Same performance

### Steps to Reproduce
```python

import numpy as np
import astropy.units as u
from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord
import pandas as pd
import time as t

N = 100000
ra = np.random.uniform(1, 200, N)
de = np.random.uniform(-70, 70, N)

df = pd.DataFrame()
df['ra'], df['dec'] = ra, de

# Very slow
s = t.time()
gc = SkyCoord(ra=df['ra'] * u.degree, dec=df['dec'] * u.degree)
print(t.time() - s)

# Not slow
s = t.time()
gc = SkyCoord(ra=df['ra'].values * u.degree, dec=df['dec'].values * u.degree)
print(t.time() - s)
```

### Description
Defining a `SkyCoord`  object using a `pandas` dataframe is hundreds of times slower than simply using a `numpy` array

### Expected behavior
Same performance

### Steps to Reproduce
```python

import numpy as np
import astropy.units as u
from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord
import pandas as pd
import time as t

N = 100000
ra = np.random.uniform(1, 200, N)
de = np.random.uniform(-70, 70, N)

df = pd.DataFrame()
df['ra'], df['dec'] = ra, de

# Very slow
s = t.time()
gc = SkyCoord(ra=df['ra'] * u.degree, dec=df['dec'] * u.degree)
print(t.time() - s)

# Not slow
s = t.time()
gc = SkyCoord(ra=df['ra'].values * u.degree, dec=df['dec'].values * u.degree)
print(t.time() - s)
```

### Description
<!-- Provide a general description of the bug. -->
`io.ascii.read()` uses too much memory

### Expected behavior
<!-- What did you expect to happen. -->
A similar footprint as that of `pandas`

### Actual behavior
<!-- What actually happened. -->
<!-- Was the output confusing or poorly described? -->
Uses *way* more memory to load the exact same file compared to `pandas.read_csv()`

### Steps to Reproduce
<!-- Ideally a code example could be provided so we can run it ourselves. -->
<!-- If you are pasting code, use triple backticks (```) around
your code snippet. -->
<!-- If necessary, sanitize your screen output to be pasted so you do not
reveal secrets like tokens and passwords. -->

1. Use a somewhat large file (>500 Mb)
2. Load using `ascii.read()`
3. Load using `pd.read_csv()`
4. Compare how much memory each process employed

```python
file = "some_large_file"
data = pd.read_csv(file, index_col=False)
data = ascii.read(file)
```
